Postoperative wound dehiscence, initial encounter - Onset: 03/02/2026
Cellulitis of toe of left foot - Onset: 03/02/2026
Type 2 diabetes mellitus with diabetic peripheral angiopathy with gangrene, unspecified whether long term
Surgical & Procedure History not reviewed (last reviewed 03/02/2026)
Past Medical History not reviewed (last reviewed 03/02/2026)
Patient is a 68yoM who presents today following left partial 5th ray amputation due to gangrene on 2/13/26. He
ORTHOPEDIC FOOT AND ANKLE INSTITUTE 3175 SAINT ROSE PKWY, HENDERSON NV 89052-3500
ambulates in normal shoegear today no surgical shoe as his last one broke. No new complaints. No recent
Examination of the left lower extremity reveals lightly palpable DP/PT pulses. Gross sensation is not intact. Gross
motor function is intact. Surgical site is clean and dry. No erythema noted around the distal lateral aspect of
the incision. Incisions are approximated with dehiscence noted at the distal aspect which is now a full-thickness
wound with a fibrogranular base. No direct probe-to-bone. No active drainage. No areas of fluctuance or
Sharp, excisional debridement was performed today in the office. Debridement was carried down to, and
including, the level of subcutaneous tissue. All necrotic, fibrotic, and non-viable tissue was removed until
the wound base exhibited healthy, bleeding, granular tissue. Hemostasis was achieved with pressure to
the wound and a dressing was then applied consisting of wet to dry dressing.
68yoM, diabetic, presenting for a postoperative visit following left partial 5th ray amputation on 2/13/26.
The surgical site was evaluated and is healing with full-thickness dehiscence on the distal aspect of the incision,
without clinical sign of infection. The patient was instructed to continue WBAT in CAM boot/post op shoe. Given
the depth of the wound, I will prescribe negative pressure wound therapy (wound vac), a wound care
clinic performing vac changes MWF. Wound care instructions were reinforced, including keeping the area
clean and dry and monitoring for any signs of infection such as redness, drainage, or increasing pain. A gradual
transition plan was outlined, with anticipated local wound care until complete healing. He requested to go back
to work he states he now has a sitting scooter he can use so he does not have to walk around as much. This
is okay as long as he wears his CAM boot when he does have to ambulate. If he has any worsening symptoms,
we will likely have to keep him off work for a longer duration. The patient was advised to follow up in 2 week
for repeat evaluation. All questions were addressed, and the patient verbalized understanding of the recovery
1. Cellulitis of toe of left foot
